hero.heading: I identify what's holding your system back, before it holds your business back.
hero.subtitle: As complexity grows, decisions drift from what was agreed into how the system is actually shaped, until the business is running on a structure no one chose. I find the root cause and design the structure that enforces decisions automatically, independent of individuals, technology in use, or sheer discipline.
observations.heading.status_quo: Does any of this sound familiar?
observations.transition: The same patterns shape everyday life within an organization.
observations.1.title: Discipline Dependency
observations.1.reality: Conventions and standards that only survive as long as someone is watching.
observations.2.title: Structural Fragility
observations.2.reality: A system landscape where complexity has made every change unpredictable.
observations.3.title: Decision Drift
observations.3.reality: Decisions that exist on paper but nowhere else.
observations.4.title: Knowledge Concentration
observations.4.reality: Knowledge that lives in individual minds and disappears with them.
observations.5.title: Coordination Overhead
observations.5.reality: Individuals and teams each pulling in their own direction.
observations.6.title: Onboarding Friction
observations.6.reality: New hires need weeks or months to fully understand the system landscape.
observations.7.title: AI-Assisted Development
observations.7.reality: AI-generated code enters the system landscape faster than structure can keep up with.
services.heading: How we work together
services.subtitle: Three paths, depending on where you are.
services.diagnosis.title: Diagnosis
services.diagnosis.subtitle: Straight to the root cause
services.diagnosis.problem: The symptoms are visible, the root cause isn't. You know something needs to change, and you want the right starting point.
services.diagnosis.forwhom.1: You're dealing with a system landscape where no one has a complete overview anymore.
services.diagnosis.forwhom.2: You change one thing, and three others break, because the dependencies between them are invisible.
services.diagnosis.forwhom.3: You have decisions to make whose consequences will compound for years, and you want the ground truth before committing.
services.diagnosis.timeline.1.title: Kick-off & alignment
services.diagnosis.timeline.2.title: Investigation
services.diagnosis.timeline.2.item.1: Trace the connections between symptoms and causes.
services.diagnosis.timeline.2.item.2: Identify the root causes behind what you're seeing.
services.diagnosis.timeline.2.item.3: Conversations with key stakeholders to fill the gaps the system doesn't reveal.
services.diagnosis.timeline.3.title: Findings & handover
services.diagnosis.timeline.3.item.1: Findings report: root causes, impact, urgency
services.diagnosis.timeline.3.item.2: A clear recommendation for where to start.
services.diagnosis.delivers.1: A compact findings report, one to two pages, ready to act on.
services.diagnosis.delivers.2: Supporting evidence in the form required by the finding, for example, a dependency analysis, annotated code, a failure-mode diagram, or a process map.
services.diagnosis.delivers.3: Root causes prioritised by urgency and impact, each backed by concrete evidence from your system landscape.
services.diagnosis.delivers.4: A clear recommendation for where to start.
services.diagnosis.excludes.1: Complete architectural audit or full system mapping
services.diagnosis.objection.q: What if I already know the root cause?
services.diagnosis.objection.a: Then we pressure-test what you've already concluded, and make sure the full picture is coherent and complete before you commit to a direction.
services.guidance.title: Guidance
services.guidance.subtitle: From root cause to solution
services.guidance.problem: The root cause is in sight or within reach, the solution isn't built yet. You know what needs to change, and you want it anchored where it can't drift.
services.guidance.forwhom.1: You've made decisions and written them down, but they last only as long as someone is watching. Enforced by individuals, not by the system.
services.guidance.forwhom.2: You've noticed the system taking on a shape no one intended. Small exceptions that stuck, workarounds that became the norm.
services.guidance.forwhom.3: Decisions and changes that used to take a day now take a week, because every part of the system is interconnected.
services.guidance.timeline.1.title: Root cause
services.guidance.timeline.1.outcome: The root cause is clear before we begin.
services.guidance.timeline.1.item.1: Find or deepen the root cause
services.guidance.timeline.1.item.2: Trace the dependencies that hold it in place
services.guidance.timeline.2.title: Design
services.guidance.timeline.2.outcome: Solution defined before the first change is made.
services.guidance.timeline.2.item.2: Agree on how the changes will roll out
services.guidance.timeline.2.item.3: Align with your team before anything is built
services.guidance.timeline.3.title: Implementation
services.guidance.timeline.3.outcome: Your team implements, I make sure we're on the right track.
services.guidance.timeline.3.item.1: Your team brings their system landscape expertise
services.guidance.timeline.3.item.2: One change at a time, until the root cause is resolved
services.guidance.timeline.3.item.3: Nothing moves forward until the previous change is locked in
services.guidance.timeline.4.title: Completion
services.guidance.timeline.4.outcome: The root cause is resolved. Drift surfaces the moment it happens.
services.guidance.timeline.4.item.1: Closing session confirming the root cause is resolved
services.guidance.timeline.4.item.2: The solution is in production and enforcing itself
services.guidance.delivers.2: Standards and conventions the system enforces automatically
services.guidance.delivers.3: An implementation that stays aligned from start to finish
services.guidance.delivers.4: A solution that outlasts individuals, tools, and sheer discipline
services.guidance.objection.q: We don't have an in-house development team, does that still work?
services.guidance.objection.a: Yes. I also work with external teams or freelancers you're working with on the implementation.
services.advisory.title: Advisory
services.advisory.subtitle: Alignment that grows with your system
services.advisory.problem: The decisions are yours, the time to dig into each one isn't. You want someone who thinks with you at the system level, and you want them reachable when you need them.
services.advisory.forwhom.1: Your system landscape keeps growing, and the decisions get harder with it.
services.advisory.forwhom.2: Small decisions today compound into structural consequences tomorrow, and you're making them without a second pair of eyes.
services.advisory.forwhom.3: Every structural decision lands on your desk, and you want someone to think it through with you.
services.advisory.timeline.1.title: Onboarding
services.advisory.timeline.1.outcome: By the end of the month, I know what matters.
services.advisory.timeline.1.item.1: Work through the system landscape with you
services.advisory.timeline.1.item.2: Understand the standards and goals currently in play
services.advisory.timeline.1.item.3: Establish the working rhythm we'll use going forward
services.advisory.timeline.2.title: Feedback & alignment
services.advisory.timeline.2.item.1: Evaluate decisions in the full system context
services.advisory.timeline.2.item.2: Grounded feedback before a decision is made
services.advisory.timeline.2.item.3: The rhythm adapts to what's on your desk
services.advisory.timeline.3.title: Deep dive
services.advisory.timeline.3.item.1: Evaluate what changed and what it means for the direction
services.advisory.delivers.2: Ongoing alignment that grows as your system evolves
services.advisory.delivers.3: A dedicated partner who knows the context that matters
services.advisory.delivers.4: Confidence in decisions that reach beyond your core expertise
services.advisory.objection.q: How long until you really know my system landscape?
services.advisory.objection.a: That depends on the complexity. In the first month I get up to speed, after that I know the context that matters.
about.heading: Philipp Höllinger
calltoaction.heading: Does any of this resonate?
calltoaction.subtitle: Then get in touch.
about.who.heading: Founder, Curfee e.U. · Austria
about.who.1: I recognize patterns even before they become obvious. When I analyze a system, within minutes I can identify the areas that will later become critical, even before I can put my thoughts into words. I'm only satisfied when decisions no longer depend on individuals enforcing them. That's exactly what drives me.
about.who.2: At its core, it's a passion for the things that go unnoticed: the small details that shape the future of a system.
about.who.3: My career began in logistics: I coordinated workflows between goods, people, and processes. What shaped me: When people with different approaches have to work together, clear guidelines and reliable structures are essential so that processes run smoothly and everyone can rely on them.
about.who.3b: The need for structure becomes even more urgent in the age of AI. AI sees only what fits within its context window and does not bring its own structure to the table. It has no framework within which to operate, it follows no guidelines, and it certainly does not respect boundaries. The system has to enforce these boundaries itself, and that only works when the structure behind it is reliable.
about.who.4: After completing my degree in computer science, I moved into software development and then into architecture, spanning the automotive industry, the public sector, and software consulting. Architecture was the logical next step. I've always wanted to keep an eye on both the big picture and the small details at the same time, because one isn't complete without the other. At some point, something became clear to me that has stayed with me ever since. The reliability that was a given in logistics is still the exception, not the rule, in software systems and the organizations that build them.
about.who.5: The more tools, processes, and people interact with one another, the more important it becomes for structures to enforce decisions automatically. This applies equally to software, infrastructure, and organizational structures. Yet this rarely happens. Structure often emerges organically rather than by design, because guidelines that enforce themselves inside the system are rarely planned from the start.
about.who.5b: Dedicated roles that carry this responsibility are rare. Instead, committed individuals step in and take on this task alongside their regular duties, with considerable effort, but without the support or time to integrate their work sustainably into the system.
about.who.6: So organisations and their engineers can spend time and money on work that matters, instead of holding the system together by hand. Structural decisions belong anchored in the system itself, enforcing themselves, independent of individuals, technology used, and sheer discipline.
observations.1.potential: Conventions and standards that enforce themselves.
observations.2.potential: A system landscape where every change stays predictable as it grows.
observations.3.potential: Decisions anchored in the system itself.
observations.4.potential: Context that stays in the organization when people move on.
observations.5.potential: Individuals and teams whose work compounds instead of colliding.
observations.6.potential: New hires who spend their first weeks contributing, not deciphering.
observations.7.potential: A system landscape that absorbs AI-generated code without losing its shape.
faq.heading: Frequently asked questions
faq.subtitle: Your question isn't here? Bring it to the first conversation.
faq.patterns.structural.question: What patterns do you see in system landscapes?
faq.patterns.structural.answer:
Patterns about the system itself, and the potential inside each:

  • Discipline Dependency → Decision Enforcement: conventions and standards that only survive as long as someone is watching become conventions and standards that enforce themselves.
  • Structural Fragility → Structural Integrity: a system landscape where complexity has made every change unpredictable becomes one where every change stays predictable as it grows.
  • Decision Drift → Embedded Decisions: decisions that exist on paper but nowhere else become decisions embedded in the system itself.
  • AI-Assisted Development → AI by Design: AI-generated code entering faster than structure can keep up with becomes a system landscape that absorbs AI-generated code without losing its shape.
faq.patterns.operational.question: What patterns do you see in teams and organizations?
faq.patterns.operational.answer:
Patterns about how the people working inside the system experience it, and the potential inside each:

  • Knowledge Concentration → Shared Understanding: knowledge that lives in individual minds and disappears with them becomes context that stays in the organization when people move on.
  • Coordination Overhead → Shared Foundation: individuals and teams each pulling in their own direction become individuals and teams whose work compounds instead of colliding.
  • Onboarding Friction → Self-Guided Onboarding: new hires needing weeks or months to understand the system landscape become new hires who spend their first weeks contributing, not deciphering.
faq.decisions.question: What kinds of decisions are you talking about?
faq.decisions.answer: The Decision Enforcement Principle applies to anything that shapes how a system landscape evolves: conventions (naming, structure, layering), standards (what must hold for every service, module, or pipeline), guidelines (how the team applies those standards in edge cases), and guardrails (the automated rules that catch inconsistencies before they reach production). The same applies to organizational systems: role definitions, escalation paths, decision rights, and process boundaries are also structural decisions that need to be anchored where they actually take effect, not only in policy documents. I treat all of these as decisions that must be anchored at a concrete point in the system, not only in documentation.
faq.enforcement.question: How do standards actually enforce themselves?
faq.enforcement.answer: Standards enforce themselves through any mechanism in the tooling landscape that runs without human intervention: CI/CD pipelines, linters, pre-commit hooks, automated tests, infrastructure as code, policy as code, schema validation, and tool configurations. In organizational systems, the same principle applies through different mechanisms: workflow automation, role definitions, escalation paths, decision-rights matrices, and onboarding pipelines. The specific mechanisms vary by system landscape; the principle stays the same. Inconsistencies surface automatically, the moment they happen, before they reach production. This is what I call the Decision Enforcement Principle.
faq.ai.question: How do you think about AI tools in this work?
faq.ai.answer: AI coding tools accelerate the exact challenge I address. An AI model only sees what fits inside its context window and brings no structure of its own, no frame, no guardrails, no boundaries. The system landscape has to enforce them. Without clear structure, AI-generated code enters a codebase faster than anyone can make sense of it.
faq.cultural.question: What if the issue turns out to be cultural, not structural?
faq.cultural.answer: Culture does not fix structure. Most of what looks cultural is individuals compensating for gaps the system leaves open. Once the structure enforces decisions, people stop carrying that weight, and what looked cultural tends to shift on its own. If the root cause genuinely is something else, the Diagnosis surfaces it within the first engagement.
faq.1.question: Who do you typically work with?
faq.1.answer: I work with decision-makers responsible for a system landscape, whether software, infrastructure, or the organization itself, where complexity has overtaken clarity: CEOs, CTOs, COOs, founders, and team leaders building companies where decisions must withstand leadership changes, tool migrations, and team growth. The common thread is an environment that has grown organically over the years, where dependencies are difficult to trace and the root causes of recurring problems are not yet obvious.
faq.2.question: What happens in the first conversation?
faq.2.answer: The first meeting is completely informal. You tell me what's on your mind; I listen to you. By the end of the meeting, both of us will know what the next step is.
faq.4.question: Do you build the solution yourself?
faq.4.answer: No. I design the solution and guide your team through the implementation process. The actual implementation is left to those who are most familiar with the system landscape. This division of labor ensures that the company's expertise remains in-house and that the results will endure even after the project is completed.
faq.keep.question: What do I keep after the engagement ends?
faq.keep.answer:
Everything. The insights, the design, the standards, the implementation: it's all yours. Nothing I've designed depends on me still being around afterward. The goal is a self-sustaining system so that you won't need any further support.

  • Discipline Dependency → Decision Enforcement: Conventions and standards that endure only as long as someone enforces them become conventions and standards that enforce themselves.
  • Structural fragility → Structural Integrity: A system landscape in which complexity has made every change unpredictable becomes one in which every change remains predictable even as complexity increases.
  • Decision drift → Embedded Decisions: Decisions that exist only on paper become decisions that are embedded within the system itself.
  • AI-driven development → AI by Design: AI-generated code that accumulates faster than the structure can keep up becomes a system landscape that incorporates AI-generated code without losing its shape.
faq.5.question: Do you work remotely or on-site?
faq.5.answer: Both. Most of my work happens remotely, with on-site visits when the situation calls for it. The service model is remote-first, and the analysis itself does not require constant physical presence.
faq.international.question: Do you only work in the DACH region?
faq.international.answer: I'm based in Salzburg, Austria, and work primarily across the DACH region (Germany, Austria, Switzerland). Most engagements are remote, so geography is less of a constraint than it used to be. I work in German and English. Teams outside DACH that share one of those languages and a workable timezone are a fit as well.
faq.6.question: Do you work inside sprints or agile frameworks?
faq.6.answer: No. Structural change does not fit inside a sprint. I work alongside the team's rhythm, but the analysis and design of structural decisions need more depth and undivided focus than a single iteration allows.

privacy.heading: Privacy Policy
privacy.phone.label: Phone
privacy.scope: This privacy policy informs users of this website, in accordance with Art. 13 of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), about the nature, scope, and purpose of the processing of personal data by the data controller.
privacy.controller.heading: Data controller
privacy.personal.heading: Processing of personal data
privacy.personal: Personal data is collected on this website only to the extent that is technically necessary. Processing is subject to purpose limitation and data minimisation. Collected data is under no circumstances disclosed to third parties without a legal basis. The processing of personal data is based on the following legal grounds: Consent pursuant to Art. 6(1)(a) GDPR, the data subject has given consent to the processing of their personal data for one or more specific purposes. Performance of a contract pursuant to Art. 6(1)(b) GDPR, processing is necessary for the performance of a contract to which the data subject is party, or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject prior to entering into a contract. Legitimate interests pursuant to Art. 6(1)(f) GDPR, processing is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject.
privacy.rights.heading: Rights of data subjects
privacy.rights: As a data subject, you have the following rights: Right of access (Art. 15 GDPR), You have the right to obtain confirmation as to whether personal data is being processed, as well as information about such data and its processing. Right to rectification (Art. 16 GDPR), You have the right to request the rectification of inaccurate or incomplete personal data. Right to erasure (Art. 17 GDPR), You have the right to request the erasure of your personal data, subject to the conditions laid down in Art. 17 GDPR. Right to restriction of processing (Art. 18 GDPR), You have the right to request the restriction of processing of your personal data. Right to data portability (Art. 20 GDPR), You have the right to receive your personal data in a structured, commonly used, and machine-readable format. Right to object (Art. 21 GDPR), You have the right to object at any time, on grounds relating to your particular situation, to the processing of personal data concerning you.
privacy.withdrawal.heading: Right to withdraw consent
privacy.withdrawal: Pursuant to Art. 7(3) GDPR, you have the right to withdraw consent at any time. The lawfulness of processing carried out on the basis of consent before its withdrawal is not affected. Withdrawal can be declared by email.
privacy.complaint.heading: Right to lodge a complaint
privacy.complaint: If you believe that the processing of your personal data violates the GDPR, you have the right, pursuant to Art. 77 GDPR, to lodge a complaint with the competent supervisory authority.
privacy.ssl.heading: SSL/TLS encryption
privacy.ssl: For security reasons and to protect the transmission of personal data, this website uses SSL or TLS encryption. An encrypted connection can be identified by the address beginning with 'https://' in the browser's address bar.
privacy.cookies.heading: Cookies and stored data
privacy.cookies: This website does not collect any cookies and does not store any data about its visitors. There is no tracking, no profiling, and no third-party scripts that would set cookies or persist information on your device. The only data processed is what is technically required to deliver the page (see the Hosting section below).
privacy.links.heading: Links to third-party websites
privacy.links: This website contains links to third-party websites. When accessing these external links, data may be transmitted to the respective operator. The controller accepts no liability for the content and data processing by third parties. Users are encouraged to review the privacy policies of linked websites.
privacy.hosting.heading: Hosting
privacy.hosting: This website is hosted on GitHub Pages, a service provided by GitHub, Inc., 88 Colin P Kelly Jr St, San Francisco, CA 94107, USA. When accessing this website, information is automatically recorded in so-called server log files. This includes: IP address, date and time of access, requested resources, browser and operating system used. This data is stored temporarily for security purposes and deleted after a short period. Processing is based on the legitimate interest pursuant to Art. 6(1)(f) GDPR to ensure the uninterrupted operation of the website. For the transfer of personal data to the USA (third country), GitHub employs standard contractual clauses pursuant to Art. 46(2)(c) GDPR. GitHub is also certified under the EU-US Data Privacy Framework.
